headline: Low-Cost Antidepressant Shows Promise Against Long COVID Fatigue
meta_description: A clinical trial found that fluvoxamine, a common antidepressant, significantly reduced fatigue in long COVID patients, offering the first evidence-based medic…
summary_short: Researchers discovered that fluvoxamine, an affordable antidepressant, meaningfully eased long COVID fatigue in a 399-person trial—a major breakthrough for millions.
body:
For millions struggling with long COVID, fatigue can mean the difference between working and staying home, between managing daily tasks and collapsing into bed. A new clinical trial offers concrete hope.

Scientists from McMaster University, Stanford, Duke, and Brazilian institutions tested fluvoxamine—a medication already widely used to treat depression—in 399 adults experiencing persistent fatigue at least 90 days after COVID infection. Over 60 days, those who received fluvoxamine reported significantly reduced exhaustion and improved quality of life compared to those given a placebo. The statistical evidence was compelling: a 99 percent probability the medication worked.

What makes this finding remarkable isn't just the results. Fluvoxamine is inexpensive and already available worldwide, meaning it could reach patients quickly. The trial also employed cutting-edge methodology called Bayesian adaptive design, which allowed researchers to reach reliable conclusions faster than traditional approaches—ending the fluvoxamine group early once evidence became clear.

Researchers caution that fluvoxamine isn't a complete solution. Long COVID involves many symptoms and biological pathways, and the drug appears specifically effective for fatigue. With 65 million people estimated to have long COVID globally, more research is needed to understand which patients benefit most.

But for the first time, clinicians have strong evidence for a medication that helps. As one researcher noted, patients want something they can try today—and this finding brings that reality closer.
